html:
Every Jamaican employer must file an annual NHT (National Housing Trust) return by March 31, 2026. The NHT annual return summarises all NHT contributions — both employee and employer — that you deducted and remitted throughout the 2025 calendar year. Filing late or inaccurately exposes you to NHT penalties and can affect your employees' ability to access NHT benefits. This guide covers everything you need to file correctly and on time.

Related Resources
• Payroll Software Jamaica — generates NHT annual return data automatically
• Free Payroll Calculator — verify NHT deductions before filing
• Small Business Payroll Jamaica — affordable compliance tools for Jamaican SMEs

What is the NHT Annual Return?

The National Housing Trust (NHT) annual return is a statutory filing that reconciles all NHT contributions you collected and remitted throughout the prior year. It shows each employee's name, NHT number, total earnings, and the NHT contributions paid on their behalf. The NHT uses this data to credit individual accounts — your employees cannot access their NHT entitlements (for home loans, grants, or refunds) unless your contributions are correctly reported and attributed to their accounts.

Unlike the TAJ SO1 return (which covers PAYE and NIS), the NHT annual return is filed directly with the National Housing Trust. Both share the March 31 deadline, but they are completely separate filings.

Who Must File the NHT Annual Return?

All employers registered with the NHT who had at least one employee during the prior calendar year. This includes:

  • Companies, sole traders, and partnerships
  • Non-profit and charitable organisations with paid staff
  • Employers of part-time and casual workers
  • Employers of domestic workers earning above the NHT threshold

NHT registration is mandatory for all employers in Jamaica. If you employed anyone during 2025, you must file — regardless of whether all employees were low earners.

NHT Contribution Rates for 2026

NHT contributions for 2026 are:

  • Employee contribution: 2% of gross emoluments (no ceiling)
  • Employer contribution: 3% of gross emoluments (no ceiling)

Unlike NIS, there is no insurable wage ceiling for NHT — contributions apply to the full gross salary no matter how high. This means NHT contributions on executive and high-earning staff can be significant, and getting them right matters.

What Goes on the NHT Annual Return?

For each employee who worked for you during 2025:

  • Full name
  • NHT number — mandatory for crediting contributions to individual accounts
  • TRN (Tax Registration Number)
  • Total gross emoluments paid during the year
  • Total employee NHT contributions deducted (2% of gross)
  • Total employer NHT contributions paid (3% of gross)
  • Number of contribution periods (weeks or months)

The return also requires employer-level totals across all employees, which must reconcile to your 12 monthly NHT remittances.

How to File the NHT Annual Return

Option 1: NHT Online Portal

The NHT accepts electronic filings through their online portal. This is the fastest method and generates immediate confirmation of receipt. Employers with more than 10 employees are strongly encouraged to file electronically.

Option 2: In-Person at NHT Offices

Paper returns can be submitted at any NHT parish office. If filing by mail, use registered mail to confirm receipt before the deadline.

What You Need Before Filing

  • Your NHT employer registration number
  • Payroll records for all 12 months of 2025
  • All 12 monthly NHT remittance receipts for reconciliation
  • A complete list of employees with their NHT numbers and TRNs

Reconciling Your NHT Annual Return

Before filing, reconcile your annual NHT total against your 12 monthly remittance receipts. Your total annual NHT contributions (employee + employer) should match the sum of your monthly payments. Common reconciliation issues include:

  • Months where remittances were made late or missed entirely
  • Employees who joined or left mid-year and were accidentally included for the wrong periods
  • Calculation errors from manual payroll where NHT was applied to net rather than gross pay
  • New employees whose NHT numbers were not collected at the start of employment

Resolve discrepancies before filing. If you remitted too little, the NHT annual return will reveal the shortfall — proactively paying arrears before March 31 reduces penalty exposure.

Penalties for Non-Compliance

Failing to file the NHT annual return, filing late, or under-reporting contributions carries consequences:

  • Late filing: Penalties apply from April 1, 2026
  • Under-reported contributions: You are liable for the shortfall plus interest
  • Employee impact: Employees cannot access NHT benefits — home loans, grants, or refunds — for periods where your contributions are not properly recorded on their NHT account

Common NHT Filing Mistakes

  • Wrong or missing NHT numbers: Contributions cannot be credited to the employee's account without their NHT number. Collect NHT numbers at hiring.
  • Applying NHT to net instead of gross pay: NHT is calculated on gross emoluments, not take-home pay. Using net figures under-deducts and under-remits.
  • Confusing NHT with SO1: The NHT annual return and the TAJ SO1 are separate filings with different authorities. Both are due March 31 — file both.
  • Omitting casual and part-time staff: All workers paid during the year must appear on the NHT return.
  • Not collecting NHT numbers for new hires: Employees hired during the year who don't have an NHT number must be assisted in registering before their contributions can be filed.

NHT Annual Return vs SO1: What's the Difference?

ItemNHT Annual ReturnSO1 / TAJ Return
Filed withNational Housing Trust (NHT)Tax Administration Jamaica (TAJ)
DeadlineMarch 31, 2026March 31, 2026
CoversNHT contributions onlyPAYE, NIS, NHT, Education Tax
NHT number required?YesNot on SO1 directly
TRN required?YesYes

How Payroll Software Makes NHT Filing Easy

Payroll software built for Jamaica — like Payroll Jamaica — tracks NHT contributions for every employee throughout the year, automatically applying the correct 2% and 3% rates to gross earnings each pay period. At year-end, you generate the NHT annual return report directly: all employee NHT numbers, earnings, and contribution figures pre-populated and reconciled to your monthly remittances.

For small businesses in Jamaica that cannot afford a dedicated payroll officer, software is the most reliable way to meet the March 31 NHT deadline without errors.

Key Dates for March 2026

  • 14th of each month: Monthly NHT remittance deadline (throughout 2025)
  • February 2026: Begin reconciling 2025 NHT records against remittance receipts
  • Early March 2026: File NHT annual return and SO1/SO2 together
  • March 31, 2026: Hard deadline for both NHT annual return and SO1

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need to file if my employees earned very little?

Yes, if they earned any NHT-eligible income and you collected contributions during the year, you must file. There is no minimum earnings threshold for the NHT annual return obligation.

What if an employee doesn't have an NHT number?

Assist them in registering with the NHT. Employers are responsible for ensuring their workforce is registered before contributions are filed. Contributions without a valid NHT number cannot be credited to the employee's account.

Can I file the NHT and SO1 returns at the same time?

They are separate filings with different authorities — NHT to the National Housing Trust, SO1/SO2 to TAJ. They share the March 31 deadline, but you must complete and submit each one separately. Plan to file both in the same week to avoid missing either deadline.
